The new centre of buoyancy $B'$ lies at the centroid of the residual (non-flooded) underwater volume. For a *symmetrically* flooded centre compartment with negligible change of trim, the new $KB'$ can be obtained by the *lost* buoyancy correction.

**Lever arm of the lost buoyancy about the original waterline** $z_{lost}$ (positive upward) is found from the centroid of the flooded volume relative to WL.

$$z_B' = \dfrac{\nabla \cdot z_B - \mu V_f \cdot z_{lost}}{\nabla - \mu V_f}$$

The new metacentre is:

$$KM' = KB' + \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla - \mu V_f}$$

where $I_T$ is the transverse second moment of the new waterplane (approximately the intact $I_T$ for small sinkage).

The new vertical centre of gravity:

$$KG' = \dfrac{W \cdot KG + w_{add} \cdot z_{add}}{W + w_{add}}$$

where $z_{add}$ is the centroid of the water *in the flooded compartment* (positive upward from baseline).

Free-surface effect of the flooded water (treated as a free liquid inside the hull, e.g., partially filled cargo hold):

$$GG' = \dfrac{i \cdot \rho}{W + w_{add}}$$

where $i$ is the transverse free-surface moment of the flooded waterplane inside the compartment.

Finally:

$$\boxed{GM' = KM' - KG' + GG'}$$

Positive $GM'$ is mandatory.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Worked-Out Solution)

**Given** (from a typical KTU lab problem statement):

- $L_{pp} = 150$ m, $B = 22$ m, $D = 13$ m
- Subdivision draft $T_0 = 8.5$ m
- Block coefficient $C_B = 0.72$, $C_W = 0.78$, $C_M = 0.95$
- Permeability of cargo hold $\mu = 0.70$
- Margin line = 75 mm below the upper deck

**Step 1 — Original displacement**:

$$\nabla_0 = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T_0$$
$$\nabla_0 = 0.72 \times 150 \times 22 \times 8.5$$
$$\nabla_0 = 20{,}196\ \text{m}^3$$

$$W_0 = \nabla_0 \cdot \rho = 20{,}196 \times 1.025 = 20{,}700.9\ \text{t}$$

**Step 2 — Waterplane area**:

$$A_W = C_W \cdot L \cdot B = 0.78 \times 150 \times 22 = 2{,}574\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Flooded volume per metre of compartment length at midship**:

The cross-sectional area at midship is:

$$A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T_0 = 0.95 \times 22 \times 8.5 = 177.65\ \text{m}^2$$

For a compartment of length $\ell$ at midship, $V_f = A_M \cdot \ell$ (good first-order approximation):

$$V_f = 177.65 \cdot \ell\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Sinkage using LBM**:

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{\mu V_f}{A_W} = \dfrac{0.70 \times 177.65 \cdot \ell}{2{,}574}$$
$$\Delta T = 0.04830 \cdot \ell\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Margin line constraint**:

The margin line is at height $D - 0.075 = 12.925$ m above the baseline. Hence:

$$T_0 + \Delta T \le 12.925$$
$$8.5 + 0.04830 \cdot \ell \le 12.925$$
$$0.04830 \cdot \ell \le 4.425$$
$$\ell \le 91.61\ \text{m}$$

> **Margin-line-limited floodable length ≈ 91.6 m**

**Step 6 — Residual stability check at $\ell = 91.6$ m**:

Centroid of flooded volume above baseline ≈ $T_0/2 = 4.25$ m.
Effective lost buoyancy per unit length (in displacement terms):
$$w_{add} = \mu V_f \rho = 0.70 \times 177.65 \times 91.6 \times 1.025 = 11{,}680\ \text{t}$$
$$W_{new} = 20{,}700.9 + 11{,}680 = 32{,}381\ \text{t}$$

New $KG$ (assuming $KG = 8.0$ m, centroid of water in flooded compartment $z_{add} = 4.25$ m):

$$KG' = \dfrac{20{,}700.9 \times 8.0 + 11{,}680 \times 4.25}{32{,}381} = 6.65\ \text{m}$$

Transverse $I_T$ at original WL (approximate): $I_T = L \cdot B^3/12 = 150 \times 22^3/12 = 133{,}100\ \text{m}^4$

$$KB' \approx 0.53 T_{new} = 0.53 \times 12.0 = 6.36\ \text{m (approx.)}$$
$$KM' = KB' + \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla_0 - \mu V_f} = 6.36 + \dfrac{133{,}100}{20{,}196 - 11{,}395} = 6.36 + 15.13 = 21.49\ \text{m}$$
$$GM' = 21.49 - 6.65 = 14.84\ \text{m}$$

GM' is positive and large — margin line is the controlling criterion for this large compartment length.

**Step 7 — Iteratively reduce $\ell$ until the smaller of the two constraints dominates**:

- For smaller $\ell$, the change in $KG'$ is small and $GM'$ remains positive.
- The **margin line criterion** is therefore the binding one: **$\ell_{floodable} \approx 91.6$ m** at midship for this loading condition.

**Step 8 — Verify that $\ell_{floodable}$ is also $\le$ the SOLAS permissible length**:

For $L = 150$ m cargo ship, $F \approx 0.40$ (interpolated):

$$\ell_{perm} = F \cdot L = 0.40 \times 150 = 60\ \text{m}$$

So the **permissible length** of 60 m is well below the **margin-line floodable length** of 91.6 m — the SOLAS stability criterion is the binding one. The actual bulkhead spacing must therefore be ≤ 60 m.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Floodable Length at Midship** (governed by SOLAS residual stability, not by margin line): **$\ell_{max} = 60\ \text{m}$** for a 150 m cargo ship with the assumed $C_B$ and $T_0$.

The process is repeated at stations $x = 0.1L,\ 0.2L, \dots$ and the **floodable length curve** is plotted.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Governing Criterion

| Criterion | Symbol | SOLAS Minimum | Governing when... |
|---|---|---|---|
| Margin line not submerged | $T_{new} \le 12.925$ m | — | Large $V_f$, deep $T_0$ |
| Range of positive $GZ'$ | $\phi_{range}$ | $\ge 20°$ | Heel due to off-centre flooding |
| Peak $GZ'$ value | $GZ'_{max}$ | $\ge 0.10$ m | Very small residual $GM'$ |
| Area under $GZ'$ curve | $A_{GZ'}$ | $\ge 0.0175$ m·rad | Wide residual $GZ'$ curve but low peak |
| Positive $GM'$ | $GM'$ | $> 0$ | Single-compartment flooding near midship |
| $f \ge F$ (subdivision) | $f$ | $\ge F$ | Carriage of subdivision plan |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Floodable Length Methodology Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Ship Principal Particulars L, B, D, T0, CB, CW, CM, KG] --> B[Select Subdivision Draft T0 and Margin Line D minus 75 mm]
    B --> C[For each station xi from FP to AP]
    C --> D[Assume candidate compartment length ℓ centred at xi]
    D --> E[Compute flooded geometric volume Vf using Bonde area curve or CM × B × T0 × ℓ]
    E --> F[Apply permeability μ to get effective volume μVf]
    F --> G[Compute sinkage ΔT = μVf / AW using LBM]
    G --> H[Compute new draft Tnew = T0 + ΔT]
    H --> I{Margin Line Submerged?}
    I -- Yes --> X1[Reduce ℓ and return to D]
    I -- No --> J[Compute added weight wadd = μ Vf ρ]
    J --> K[Compute new KG prime and KM prime]
    K --> L[Compute residual GM prime = KM prime minus KG prime plus GG prime]
    L --> M{Residual GM prime greater than 0 and SOLAS minima met?}
    M -- No --> X2[Reduce ℓ and return to D]
    M -- Yes --> N[Record ℓi as floodable length at xi]
    N --> O{All stations completed?}
    O -- No --> C
    O -- Yes --> P[Plot floodable length curve ℓx vs x]
    P --> Q[Compare with actual bulkhead spacing]
    Q --> R[If actual spacing exceeds curve, add watertight bulkhead]
    R --> S[End: Verify factor f greater than or equal to F]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Section of Flooded Compartment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Ship Section
        WL0[Original Waterline WL0] --> WL1[New Waterline WL1 after flooding]
        WL1 -.-> ML[Margin Line 75 mm below deck]
        ML --> DK[Upper Deck]
        DK --> SH[Sheer Line]
        WL1 --> H1[Flooded Compartment Length ℓ]
        H1 --> H2[Watertight Bulkheads]
        WL0 -.-> AW[Waterplane Area AW]
        H1 -.-> VF[Effective Flooded Volume μ Vf]
        VF -.-> DT[Sinkage ΔT]
    end
```

### 4.3 Typical Floodable Length Curve (Sketch)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Floodable Length Curve (ℓx vs x)"
    x-axis "Station x (m from FP)" [0, 15, 30, 45, 60, 75, 90, 105, 120, 135, 150]
    y-axis "Floodable Length ℓx (m)" 0 --> 120
    line [22, 38, 55, 68, 78, 84, 86, 82, 72, 56, 28]
```

> The curve is **symmetric** about midship for a single-screw cargo ship, with smaller floodable lengths at the ends (because the hull cross-section narrows there) and maximum near amidships.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**  
> *"Define floodable length. Why is the margin line taken 75 mm below the freeboard deck?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

- **Definition (2 marks):** Floodable length is the maximum length of a watertight compartment, centred at a given point, that may be flooded under defined damage conditions without the ship sinking below the margin line and without the residual GZ curve falling below SOLAS minima.
- **Margin line explanation (1 mark):** The 75 mm offset provides a *safety reserve* against wave action, plating deflection, and inaccuracies in the damage scenario, ensuring that the uppermost watertight envelope is not breached by the rising waterline in seaway.

#### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**  
> *"Distinguish between the lost buoyancy method and the added weight method of floodable length calculation."*

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Aspect | Lost Buoyancy Method | Added Weight Method |
|---|---|---|
| Concept | Volume of flooded space is treated as missing buoyancy | Water in the flooded space is treated as added weight |
| Equilibrium | $\nabla(T_0) = \nabla(T_{new}) - \mu V_f$ | $W + w_{add} = \rho \nabla(T_{new})$ |
| Best suited for | Small compartments (residual volume dominates) | Large compartments (added weight dominates) |
| Output | $\Delta T = \mu V_f / A_W$ | Same in linear regime |

### 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based 14-Mark Questions

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

#### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**  
> A bulk carrier has the following particulars: $L_{pp} = 145$ m, $B = 22.5$ m, $D = 13.5$ m, subdivision draft $T_0 = 8.0$ m, $C_B = 0.74$, $C_W = 0.78$, $C_M = 0.96$, $KG = 7.5$ m, permeability of cargo hold $\mu = 0.70$.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the floodable length at midship using the lost buoyancy method, stating the margin-line constraint clearly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** If the ship is to comply with SOLAS Regulation 4 for a cargo ship (factor of subdivision $F = 0.45$ for $L = 145$ m), determine the permissible compartment length and check whether the actual bulkhead spacing of 50 m complies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]**:

**Step 1 — Displacement volume** (1 mark):

$$\nabla_0 = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T_0 = 0.74 \times 145 \times 22.5 \times 8.0 = 19{,}314\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Waterplane area** (1 mark):

$$A_W = C_W \cdot L \cdot B = 0.78 \times 145 \times 22.5 = 2{,}545.5\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Flooded volume per metre of compartment at midship** (1 mark):

$$A_M = C_M \cdot B \cdot T_0 = 0.96 \times 22.5 \times 8.0 = 172.8\ \text{m}^2$$

$$V_f = 172.8 \cdot \ell\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Sinkage** (1 mark):

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{\mu V_f}{A_W} = \dfrac{0.70 \times 172.8 \cdot \ell}{2{,}545.5} = 0.0475 \cdot \ell\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Margin line check** (2 marks):

The margin line is at $13.5 - 0.075 = 13.425$ m. Therefore:

$$T_0 + \Delta T \le 13.425 \;\Rightarrow\; 8.0 + 0.0475 \ell \le 13.425 \;\Rightarrow\; \ell \le 114.2\ \text{m}$$

**Floodable length at midship, margin-line limited = 114.2 m**.

**Step 6 — Residual stability check** (1 mark):

For the same $L$, the SOLAS permissible length from part (b) will be calculated as 65.25 m, which is lower than 114.2 m, so SOLAS stability governs.

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]**:

**Step 1 — Required factor of subdivision** (1 mark):

For $L = 145$ m (cargo ship, no passengers): from SOLAS Reg. 4 Table 4.1.1, linear interpolation between $L = 130$ m ($F = 0.48$) and $L = 150$ m ($F = 0.43$):

$$F = 0.48 + (0.43 - 0.48) \times \dfrac{145 - 130}{150 - 130} = 0.48 - 0.0375 = 0.4425 \approx 0.44$$

Given $F = 0.45$ in the problem (a slightly conservative reading).

**Step 2 — Permissible compartment length** (2 marks):

$$\ell_{perm} = F \cdot L = 0.45 \times 145 = 65.25\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Compliance check** (2 marks):

Actual bulkhead spacing = 50 m. Since $50\ \text{m} < 65.25\ \text{m}$, the bulkhead spacing **complies** with the regulation.

**Step 4 — Margin line check for the actual compartment** (2 marks):

At $\ell = 50$ m, $\Delta T = 0.0475 \times 50 = 2.375$ m. New draft = 8.0 + 2.375 = 10.375 m < 13.425 m. ✓

**Conclusion**: The bulkhead spacing of 50 m is acceptable; both SOLAS and margin-line criteria are satisfied.

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**  
> For a single-screw cargo vessel of $L = 120$ m, $B = 18$ m, $D = 9$ m, subdivision draft $T_0 = 6.0$ m, $C_B = 0.70$, $C_W = 0.75$, $C_M = 0.94$, permeability of a midship cargo hold $\mu = 0.70$, and $KG = 5.5$ m:
>
> **(a)** Determine the maximum compartment length (floodable length) at midship using the **added weight method**, with the margin line 75 mm below the deck. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute the residual metacentric height $GM'$ for a compartment of length $\ell = 30$ m flooded at midship, and verify whether the SOLAS residual stability criterion (peak $GZ' \ge 0.10$ m and range $\ge 20°$) is likely met. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]**:

**Step 1 — Original displacement** (1 mark):

$$\nabla_0 = 0.70 \times 120 \times 18 \times 6.0 = 9{,}072\ \text{m}^3$$
$$W_0 = 9{,}072 \times 1.025 = 9{,}299\ \text{t}$$

**Step 2 — Waterplane area** (1 mark):

$$A_W = 0.75 \times 120 \times 18 = 1{,}620\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Flooded volume per unit length at midship** (1 mark):

$$A_M = 0.94 \times 18 \times 6.0 = 101.52\ \text{m}^2$$
$$V_f = 101.52 \cdot \ell\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Added weight and sinkage** (2 marks):

$$w_{add} = \mu \cdot V_f \cdot \rho = 0.70 \times 101.52 \ell \times 1.025 = 72.84 \ell\ \text{t}$$

Equilibrium: $W_0 + w_{add} = \rho \cdot \nabla(T_{new})$, with $A_W$ assumed constant:

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{w_{add}}{\rho A_W} = \dfrac{72.84 \ell}{1.025 \times 1{,}620} = 0.0439 \ell\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Margin line constraint** (2 marks):

$T_{new} = 6.0 + 0.0439 \ell \le 9.0 - 0.075 = 8.925$ m:

$$0.0439 \ell \le 2.925 \;\Rightarrow\; \ell \le 66.6\ \text{m}$$

**Floodable length at midship ≈ 66.6 m** (margin-line limited).

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]**:

**Step 1 — Sinkage for $\ell = 30$ m** (1 mark):

$$\Delta T = 0.0439 \times 30 = 1.317\ \text{m}, \quad T_{new} = 7.317\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — New displacement** (1 mark):

$$W_{new} = 9{,}299 + 72.84 \times 30 = 11{,}484\ \text{t}$$

**Step 3 — New $KG'$** (1 mark):

Centroid of water in flooded compartment ≈ $T_0/2 = 3.0$ m. Hence:

$$KG' = \dfrac{9{,}299 \times 5.5 + 2{,}185 \times 3.0}{11{,}484} = \dfrac{51{,}144 + 6{,}555}{11{,}484} = 5.02\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — New $KB'$ and $KM'$** (2 marks):

$KB' \approx 0.53 T_{new} = 3.88$ m

Transverse $I_T$ at original WL: $I_T = LB^3/12 = 120 \times 18^3/12 = 58{,}320\ \text{m}^4$

Residual volume $\nabla' = \nabla_0 - \mu V_f = 9{,}072 - 0.70 \times 101.52 \times 30 = 9{,}072 - 2{,}132 = 6{,}940\ \text{m}^3$

$$KM' = 3.88 + \dfrac{58{,}320}{6{,}940} = 3.88 + 8.40 = 12.28\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Residual $GM'$** (1 mark):

Neglecting free-surface correction: $GM' = 12.28 - 5.02 = 7.26$ m (large positive).

**Step 6 — Verification** (1 mark):

The very large $GM'$ implies a stiff ship. The peak $GZ'$ in the residual curve will be much greater than 0.10 m and the range of positive stability will exceed 20°. The SOLAS criteria are **comfortably met**. A full GZ computation by integration of the cross-section curve is required for the final compliance report.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (commonly lost marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 75 mm offset** when locating the margin line — examiners deduct 1 mark consistently.
> 2. **Mixing up permeability**: cargo holds 0.70, machinery spaces 0.85, accommodation 0.95. Wrong value of $\mu$ ruins the calculation.
> 3. **Not stating units** for $\nabla$ (m³) and $W$ (t). The conversion $\rho = 1.025$ must be explicit.
> 4. **Confusing $L$ and $L_{pp}$**: the SOLAS subdivision length $L$ is sometimes 96% of the waterline length, not the perpendiculars length.
> 5. **Ignoring the factor-of-subdivision table**: the margin-line-limited length is *not* the final answer — SOLAS stability criterion (smaller $f \cdot L$) often governs. Examiners expect a comparison.
> 6. **Skipping the iteration**: stating "$\Delta T$ is proportional to $\ell$" without showing the resulting inequality $T_0 + \Delta T \le D - 0.075$ loses 1 mark.
> 7. **Wrong sign convention for $z_{add}$**: centroid of flooded water is *above* the baseline, so positive $z$ in the lever-arm equation is correct.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Floodable Length Calculation**

- ☐ **Margin line** is **75 mm (3 in) below the freeboard deck** at the side. Always subtract this offset.
- ☐ **Standard permeability** $\mu$: cargo 0.70, machinery 0.85, accommodation 0.95, stores 0.60, peaks 0.70.
- ☐ **Lost Buoyancy**: $\Delta T = \mu V_f / A_W$ (assumes constant $A_W$ — valid for small $\ell$).
- ☐ **Added Weight**: $w_{add} = \mu V_f \rho$, then $\Delta T = w_{add} / (\rho A_W)$ — identical in the linear regime.
- ☐ **Residual $GM' = KM' - KG' + GG'$**, where $GG' = i\rho / (W + w_{add})$.
- ☐ **Permissible length** $\ell_{perm} = f \cdot L$. Required: $f \ge F$ (from SOLAS Reg. 4 Table 4.1.1).
- ☐ **Two governing checks**: (i) margin line not submerged, (ii) SOLAS residual stability minima (range, peak $GZ'$, area under curve).
- ☐ **Floodable length curve** must enclose every actual compartment length at every station.
- ☐ **Densities**: seawater 1.025 t/m³, fresh water 1.000 t/m³.
- ☐ **Coefficients**: $C_B$ (block), $C_M$ (midship), $C_W$ (waterplane). Typical $C_W \approx 0.75 C_B$ to $0.85 C_B$.
- ☐ **Iteration** is essential: increase $\ell$ until a constraint is breached, then back off to the previous value.
- ☐ **Off-centre flooding** introduces heel — the standard procedure in SOLAS is to assume the *worst* damage location, often with a transverse moment of $0.5 \mu V_f$ for non-symmetric floods.
- ☐ The **floodable length at the ends** is small because the hull cross-section narrows; the maximum is near amidships.
- ☐ **Documentation**: the final subdivision plan (bulkhead arrangement) is part of the ship's Trim & Stability Booklet, reviewed by the Classification Society.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL508 - SHIP DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Damage stability computations at given loading condition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Damage Stability (KTU 2024 / IMO Definition):**
> Damage stability is the branch of naval architecture that quantifies the residual stability and survivability of a vessel after one or more compartments have been breached and flooded, at a specified **Loading Condition** (typically the deepest subdivision loadline). It is governed by the **SOLAS 1974 Convention (Chapter II-1, Part B-1, Regulation 7–8)**, the **International Code on Intact Stability 2008 (IS Code)**, and **ICLL 1966 (as amended)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme lab terminology, *Damage stability computations at a given loading condition* refer to the deterministic evaluation of a ship's post-flooding **Equilibrium Waterline (EWL)**, **Residual Righting Lever (GZ) curve**, **Range of Positive Residual Stability**, and **Factor of Subdivision**, by treating the flooded compartment as an equivalent lost-buoyancy volume filled with seawater of density **1.025 t/m³**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Drifting Cargo Raft"**
> Imagine a sealed, watertight rectangular lunch-box floating in a bathtub. If you punch a hole in one side, water rushes in and the box sinks deeper. Two things happen simultaneously: **(1) the box loses some of its original buoyant volume** (it now carries water instead of air), and **(2) its effective centre of gravity shifts** because that water is heavy and located low in one corner. The lunch-box tilts until a new equilibrium is found where the still-trapped air above the new waterline provides just enough upward push.
>
> A ship behaves identically: when a compartment is breached, the *lost buoyancy* and the *added water weight* are the two physical changes that dictate the new floating position and the new righting moment. The "lunch-box" can survive only if the *remaining intact* air-pocket volume is large enough to keep the deck-edge above water, i.e., the **Margin Line** is not submerged.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ρ_sw** | Density of seawater | **1.025 t/m³** |
| **ρ_fw** | Density of fresh water | **1.000 t/m³** |
| **T** | Mean intact draft | metres (m) |
| **Δ** | Displacement (intact) | tonnes (t) |
| **A_W** | Waterplane area (intact) | m² |
| **μ** | Permeability of compartment | 0.60 – 0.95 (dimensionless) |
| **GM** | Metacentric height (intact) | m |
| **ML** | Margin line height above WL | **0.0762 m (3 in)** for Type "A" ships per ICLL |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCNSL508 Module Exp 2):** The experiment is *deterministic* — students are given a fixed damaged compartment, fixed permeability, and a specified loading condition, and must compute the **new draft, new trim, new KG, residual GZ, and verify SOLAS residual stability criteria**. Probabilistic damage stability (FORM/IS Code Part B) is **out of scope** for this experiment.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Sequence of a Flooding Event

1. **Hull Breach** — Seawater enters the compartment through a side, bottom, or deck penetration.
2. **Progressive Flooding** — Air inside the compartment is gradually displaced through vents.
3. **Final Equilibrium** — The compartment becomes **completely flooded** to the new outside waterline (assumed in deterministic KTU computations).
4. **Lost Buoyancy** — The flooded volume can no longer support the ship; the ship sinks deeper and/or trims to find a new waterplane with sufficient intact buoyancy.
5. **Weight Penalty** — Flooded water (mass = ρ × V_flooded × μ) effectively becomes added dead-weight, shifting KG.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Equations

> **Why do we treat flooded volume as "lost buoyancy"?**  
> Because a fully flooded compartment exerts the **same hydrostatic pressure** on the surrounding hull as the seawater outside it. The net upward force contributed by the compartment to the ship becomes approximately zero — it is a "neutral" zone that adds weight but no lift. Therefore, the effective underwater hull is reduced to the *intact* volume, and the ship floats higher out of the remaining water.

> **Why is permeability (μ) used?**  
> Not all of a compartment's geometric volume fills with water — furniture, stores, machinery, and air-traps occupy space. **μ = V_water / V_compartment** scales the flooded water mass to realistic conditions. Standard SOLAS values are: Accommodation 0.95, Machinery 0.85, Cargo holds 0.60–0.70, Ballast 0.95.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation Table — Memorise These 12 Formulas for Lab/Exam**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta_{\text{new}} = \Delta_{\text{intact}} + \rho_{sw}\,\mu\,V_{\text{compt}}$ | New displacement after flooding | t |
| 2 | $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{intact}} + \dfrac{\rho_{sw}\,\mu\,V_{\text{compt}}}{A_W}$ | New mean draft (added-layer method) | m |
| 3 | $T_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{\nabla_{\text{intact}} + \mu\,V_{\text{compt}}}{A_{W,\text{new}}}$ | New draft (lost-buoyancy method, after iteration) | m |
| 4 | $KG_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{KG_{\text{intact}}\,\Delta_{\text{intact}} + k_{g,f}\,\rho_{sw}\,\mu\,V_{\text{compt}}}{\Delta_{\text{new}}}$ | New vertical CG location | m |
| 5 | $KB_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{\int_{W\!L_{\text{new}}} y\,\mathrm{d}A_{\text{intact}}}{A_{W,\text{new}}}$ | New centre of buoyancy (from intact form using new WL) | m |
| 6 | $\Delta GM = \dfrac{P\,\Delta\,V_{\text{compt}}\,(\,\text{cg of compt}\, - \text{cb of compt}\,)}{(\,\Delta + P\,V_{\text{compt}}\,)\,\Delta}$ | Loss of GM due to flooding (Reed/Taggart form) | m |
| 7 | $GG' = \dfrac{w_f}{W + w_f}\,(kg_f - KG)$ | Parallel-axis shift of G due to flood-water weight | m |
| 8 | $GZ_{\text{damaged}}(\theta) = KN_{\text{intact}}(\theta) - KG_{\text{new}} \sin\theta$ | Residual GZ at heel angle θ | m |
| 9 | $GZ_{\text{res}} = GZ_{\text{intact}} - GG'\cos\theta + \Delta GM \sin\theta$ | Combined correction (heeling + shift) | m |
| 10 | $F = \dfrac{A_{\text{required}}}{A_{\text{max}}}$ | Factor of subdivision (deterministic, SOLAS Reg. 7) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{Range} = \theta_{2} - \theta_{1} \ge 7^\circ$ | Positive residual stability range (passenger ships) | degrees |
| 12 | $\ell_{p} = \dfrac{L_{\text{ship}} \cdot (B/2)}{F \cdot (H - d_{\text{dam}})}$ | Permissible length of flooding (floodable length inverse) | m |

> **Notation Key:** $V_{\text{compt}}$ = geometric compartment volume, $k_{g,f}$ = vertical CG of flooded water (≈ centroid of compartment when fully flooded), $P$ = permeability of compartment, $A_W$ = intact waterplane area, $KN$ = lever from keel to metacentre, $\nabla$ = volume of displacement.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Regulatory Design Acceptance:** Every passenger vessel of ≥ 80 persons and every cargo ship must demonstrate survival after the worst one-compartment (or two-compartment for ro-ro) flood. The procedure here mirrors the **SOLAS s/s calculation**.
- **Watertight Subdivision Layout:** Engineers decide the number and spacing of W.T. bulkheads using floodable-length analysis.
- **Loading Manual Approval:** Surveyors (IRS, LR, DNV, BV) require residual GZ at every operational loading condition, including *ballast*, *partially loaded*, and *homogeneous-cargo* drafts.
- **Accident Investigation:** Following real casualties (e.g., *Costa Concordia*, *MV Wakashio*), the deterministic method is the first tool used to reconstruct what *should* have happened.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — New Mean Draft (Added-Layer Approximation)

This is the **primary method KTU expects** for Module Experiment 2 unless the compartment penetrates the waterplane.

We start from Archimedes' principle: the ship displaces a volume of water equal in weight to its total mass. Before damage, $\Delta_{\text{intact}} = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_{\text{intact}}$. After damage, the added flood water contributes $\Delta_{\text{add}} = \rho_{sw} \cdot \mu \cdot V_{\text{compt}}$. For a *small* change in draft, the additional displaced volume equals the added waterplane area times the change in draft:

$$
A_W \cdot \Delta T = \mu \cdot V_{\text{compt}}
$$

Solving for the change in draft:

$$
\Delta T = \frac{\mu \cdot V_{\text{compt}}}{A_W}
$$

Hence the new mean draft:

$$
T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{intact}} + \Delta T = T_{\text{intact}} + \frac{\mu \cdot V_{\text{compt}}}{A_W}
$$

> **Validity Range:** This linear approximation is accurate only when $\Delta T \ll T_{\text{intact}}$ (typically < 8 % of draft). For larger floods, iterate using the *lost-buoyancy* method (Derivation 3).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — New KG After Flooding

Treating the ship + flood water as a rigid composite body, the new vertical centre of gravity is the moment-weighted average of the intact ship and the added flood water:

$$
KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{KG_{\text{intact}} \cdot \Delta_{\text{intact}} + k_{g,f} \cdot w_f}{\Delta_{\text{intact}} + w_f}
$$

where

$$
w_f = \rho_{sw} \cdot \mu \cdot V_{\text{compt}}
$$

and $k_{g,f}$ is the vertical centroid of the *flood water* inside the compartment. For a fully-flooded compartment whose waterline equals the **outside equilibrium waterline**, the centroid is the centroid of the *submerged portion* of the compartment volume. For a horizontally symmetric compartment from the keel upward, $k_{g,f} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,T_{\text{new}}$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Loss of GM (Reed & Taggart Method)

Let $b$ be the half-beam of the intact waterplane and $a$ be the half-breadth of the damaged compartment at the new waterline. The change in waterplane moment of inertia is:

$$
\Delta I = I_{\text{removed}} = \frac{2 \cdot a^{3}\,L_{\text{compt}}}{3}
$$

The new waterplane second moment about the centreline is $I_{\text{new}} = I_{\text{intact}} - \Delta I$. New $BM_{\text{new}} = I_{\text{new}} / \nabla_{\text{new}}$. Loss of $BM$:

$$
\Delta BM = BM_{\text{intact}} - BM_{\text{new}} = \frac{I_{\text{intact}}}{\nabla_{\text{intact}}} - \frac{I_{\text{new}}}{\nabla_{\text{new}}}
$$

Now combine the **two corrections** — the increase in KG (Derivation 2) and the decrease in BM — to obtain the residual GM:

$$
GM_{\text{residual}} = GM_{\text{intact}} - \Delta BM - \Delta KG
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Complete KTU-Style Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem (Modelled on KTU 2024 Typical Lab Question):**  
> A bulk carrier has the following particulars at departure load condition:
> - $L_{pp}$ = 100 m, $B$ = 15 m, $T_{\text{intact}}$ = 5.0 m, $\Delta_{\text{intact}}$ = 8 200 t
> - $A_W$ = 1 200 m², $KG_{\text{intact}}$ = 5.6 m, $BM_{\text{intact}}$ = 2.1 m, $KB_{\text{intact}}$ = 2.6 m
> - $GM_{\text{intact}}$ = $BM - KG + KB$? — wait: $GM = KB + BM - KG$
> - Damage: side compartment No. 3, $V_{\text{compt}}$ = 800 m³, $\mu$ = 0.70, $k_{g,f}$ = 3.0 m, $I_{\text{removed}}$ = 18 000 m⁴
> - Margin line above keel = 6.0 m. Required residual stability range ≥ 7°.
>
> *Compute the new equilibrium, residual GM, residual GZ at 20°, and verify SOLAS compliance.*

**Step 1 — Mass of Flood Water**

$$
w_f = \rho_{sw} \cdot \mu \cdot V_{\text{compt}} = 1.025 \times 0.70 \times 800
$$

$$
w_f = 574 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 2 — New Displacement**

$$
\Delta_{\text{new}} = \Delta_{\text{intact}} + w_f = 8200 + 574 = 8774 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 3 — New Mean Draft (Added-Layer Method)**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{\mu \cdot V_{\text{compt}}}{A_W} = \frac{0.70 \times 800}{1200} = \frac{560}{1200}
$$

$$
\Delta T = 0.4667 \text{ m}
$$

$$
T_{\text{new}} = 5.000 + 0.4667 = 5.467 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — New KG**

$$
KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{KG_{\text{intact}} \cdot \Delta_{\text{intact}} + k_{g,f} \cdot w_f}{\Delta_{\text{new}}}
$$

$$
KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{5.6 \times 8200 + 3.0 \times 574}{8774}
$$

$$
KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{45920 + 1722}{8774} = \frac{47642}{8774}
$$

$$
KG_{\text{new}} = 5.430 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Loss of BM**

$$
BM_{\text{new}} = \frac{I_{\text{intact}} - I_{\text{removed}}}{\nabla_{\text{new}}}
$$

For $I_{\text{intact}} = BM_{\text{intact}} \cdot \nabla_{\text{intact}} = 2.1 \times 8000 = 16\,800 \text{ m}^4$ (using $\nabla_{\text{intact}} = \Delta_{\text{intact}}/\rho_{sw} = 8200/1.025 = 8000 \text{ m}^3$):

$$
I_{\text{intact}} = 16800 \text{ m}^4,\quad \nabla_{\text{new}} = \frac{8774}{1.025} = 8560 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
BM_{\text{new}} = \frac{16800 - 18000}{8560} = \frac{-1200}{8560}
$$

$$
BM_{\text{new}} = -0.140 \text{ m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative BM** indicates the damaged waterplane has its centroid *outside* the centreline due to the asymmetric flooding. In real SOLAS evaluation this is acceptable provided $KB + BM > KG$ (i.e., $KM > KG$). For pedagogical clarity, the KTU problem often assumes a symmetric flooding where $I_{\text{removed}}$ is small. Let us *re-run* with $I_{\text{removed}} = 1\,600$ m⁴ (a more realistic transverse side-compartment removal):

**Step 5 (Corrected) — $I_{\text{removed}} = 1\,600$ m⁴**

$$
BM_{\text{new}} = \frac{16800 - 1600}{8560} = \frac{15200}{8560} = 1.776 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — New KB at the New Waterline**

For a typical bulk-carrier block coefficient $C_B = 0.75$ and new draft $T_{\text{new}} = 5.467$ m, $KB_{\text{new}} \approx 0.535\,T_{\text{new}} = 2.925$ m.

**Step 7 — New (Residual) GM**

$$
GM_{\text{residual}} = KB_{\text{new}} + BM_{\text{new}} - KG_{\text{new}}
$$

$$
GM_{\text{residual}} = 2.925 + 1.776 - 5.430
$$

$$
GM_{\text{residual}} = -0.729 \text{ m}
$$

The negative value shows the ship **capsizes in this worst-case side-flood**. To pass SOLAS, the engineer would either **(a)** reduce permeability by sub-dividing, **(b)** reduce $k_{g,f}$ by adding a bilge suction and de-watering, or **(c)** restrict the loading condition (limiting KG).

**Step 8 — Margin Line Submersion Check**

Margin line at $6.0$ m above keel. The new waterline is at $5.467$ m, so the deck-edge at side remains **0.533 m above** the waterline → margin line **not submerged** (PASS).

**Step 9 — SOLAS Compliance Summary Table**

| SOLAS Criterion (Passenger/Cargo 1-compt flood) | Limit | Computed | Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Margin line not submerged | 0.076 m min clearance | +0.533 m | **PASS** |
| Range of positive residual stability (if GM > 0) | $\geq 7^\circ$ | N/A (GM < 0) | **FAIL** |
| Max GZ within 20°–30° (if GM > 0) | $\geq 0.10$ m | N/A | **FAIL** |
| Area under GZ curve to 20° / to 40° | $\geq 0.017$ / $\geq 0.023$ m·rad | N/A | **FAIL** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students stop after Step 4 and report $KG_{\text{new}}$ as the "residual stability." This is **wrong** — stability is governed by **$GM_{\text{residual}} = KB + BM - KG$**, not by KG alone. Always complete the full chain to BM and KB.

**Step 10 — Iteration Loop (if Required)**

If $\Delta T$ exceeded ~0.4 m, repeat Steps 3–7 using $A_{W,\text{new}}$ (slightly larger at deeper draft) and an updated $\nabla_{\text{new}}$. KTU problems are usually designed to converge in ≤ 2 iterations.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Damage Stability Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input: Ship Particulars L B T A_W KB KG BM Delta]
    A1[Define Damaged Compartment V_compt mu k_gf I_removed]
    B1[Compute Flood-Water Mass w_f = rho x mu x V_compt]
    B2[Compute Delta_new = Delta_intact + w_f]
    B3[Compute dT = mu x V_compt / A_W]
    B4[Compute T_new = T_intact + dT]
    C1[Compute KG_new using moment balance]
    C2[Compute I_new = I_intact - I_removed]
    C3[Compute BM_new = I_new / nabla_new]
    C4[Compute KB_new from hydrostatic curves at T_new]
    D1[Compute GM_residual = KB_new + BM_new - KG_new]
    D2[Compute GZ_damaged theta = KN_intact theta - KG_new sin theta]
    E1{Is GM_residual > 0?}
    E2[Find angle range where GZ > 0]
    E3[Check Margin Line submersion]
    F1[Check SOLAS Reg 7-8 Criteria]
    F2[Output: PASS or FAIL with redesign note]

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> E1
    E1 -- YES --> E2
    E1 -- NO --> F2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
```

### 4.2 Sectional Schematic of Flooded Compartment (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTACT_HULL
        I1[Intact Waterline WL0]
        I2[Intact Buoyancy Volume V_B]
        I3[KG_intact at centre of ship mass]
    end
    subgraph DAMAGED_ZONE
        D1[Breached W.T. Bulkhead]
        D2[Compartment No 3 V_compt 800 m3]
        D3[Seawater fills volume mu x V_compt]
    end
    subgraph NEW_EQUILIBRIUM
        N1[New Waterline WL1 deeper]
        N2[Effective Buoyancy V_B - mu V_compt]
        N3[KG_new shifted due to flood mass]
    end
    INTACT_HULL --> DAMAGED_ZONE --> NEW_EQUILIBRIUM
```

### 4.3 GZ Curve Comparison — Intact vs. Damaged

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTACT
        I1[theta 0 deg GZ 0]
        I2[theta 20 deg GZ max 0.45 m]
        I3[theta 40 deg GZ 0.32 m]
    end
    subgraph DAMAGED
        D1[theta 0 deg GZ 0]
        D2[theta 20 deg GZ max 0.15 m]
        D3[theta 30 deg GZ 0.00 m Range 30 deg]
    end
    I1 -.Negative.-> I2
    D1 -.Negative.-> D2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The damaged GZ curve lies *below* the intact curve, with a smaller peak and a narrower range. The shaded area between the curves represents the *loss of stability* due to the compartment flood.

### 4.4 SOLAS Decision Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Flooded Configuration: 1 or 2 compartments]
    S1{Margin line not submerged?}
    S2{Range of positive residual GZ ge 7 deg?}
    S3{Max GZ in 20 to 30 deg ge 0.10 m?}
    S4{Area to 20 deg ge 0.017 m.rad?}
    S5{Area from 20 to 40 deg ge 0.028 m.rad?}
    S6[CRITERIA SATISFIED Ship survives]
    S7[CRITERIA FAILED Redesign subdivision]

    S0 --> S1
    S1 -- NO --> S7
    S1 -- YES --> S2
    S2 -- NO --> S7
    S2 -- YES --> S3
    S3 -- NO --> S7
    S3 -- YES --> S4
    S4 -- NO --> S7
    S4 -- YES --> S5
    S5 -- NO --> S7
    S5 -- YES --> S6
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
> **Define "Permeability (μ)" in the context of damage stability and state its typical range for (i) a cargo hold, (ii) a machinery space.**

**Model Answer:**  
Permeability is the ratio of the volume of seawater that can occupy a compartment after flooding to the total geometric volume of that compartment, expressed as $\mu = V_{water}/V_{compt}$.  
(i) **Cargo hold:** $\mu = 0.60$ (general dry-cargo hold)  
(ii) **Machinery space:** $\mu = 0.85$  
The value depends on the amount of fittings, equipment, furniture, and air pockets that displace the flood water. [3 marks]

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
> **What is the "Margin Line" and what is its significance in damage stability?**

**Model Answer:**  
The **Margin Line** is a reference line drawn on the ship's side, **76 mm (3 in)** above the **deepest subdivision loadline** for Type A ships under ICLL 1966. After the assumed damage, the margin line **must not become submerged** at any stage of the flooding. Its significance is that it provides a **safety reserve of buoyancy** above the waterline, ensuring that the ship retains positive residual stability and a dry, accessible weather deck for evacuation. [3 marks]

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Question A (14 Marks, with internal choice)

> **Q.3(a) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, ESE Module 2 Choice A]**  
> A cargo vessel has the following departure-load particulars: $L_{pp}$ = 120 m, $B$ = 18 m, $T$ = 6.0 m, $A_W$ = 1 500 m², $\Delta$ = 12 000 t, $KG$ = 6.5 m, $KB$ = 3.1 m, $BM$ = 2.5 m. Side compartment No. 2 is breached: $V_{\text{compt}}$ = 900 m³, $\mu$ = 0.65, $k_{g,f}$ = 3.5 m, $I_{\text{removed}}$ = 2 000 m⁴. The margin line is 6.5 m above the keel.
>
> **(i)** Compute the new mean draft after flooding.  
> **(ii)** Compute the new $KG$ and the residual $GM$.  
> **(iii)** Check the SOLAS residual stability criteria and state whether the loading condition is acceptable.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) New mean draft**

$$
\Delta T = \frac{0.65 \times 900}{1500} = \frac{585}{1500} = 0.39 \text{ m}
$$

$$
T_{\text{new}} = 6.00 + 0.39 = 6.39 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) New KG**

$$
w_f = 1.025 \times 0.65 \times 900 = 599.6 \text{ t}
$$

$$
\Delta_{\text{new}} = 12000 + 599.6 = 12599.6 \text{ t}
$$

$$
KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{6.5 \times 12000 + 3.5 \times 599.6}{12599.6} = \frac{78000 + 2098.6}{12599.6} = 6.361 \text{ m}
$$

**New BM**

$$
I_{\text{intact}} = BM_{\text{intact}} \times \nabla_{\text{intact}} = 2.5 \times \frac{12000}{1.025} = 2.5 \times 11707.3 = 29268.3 \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
\nabla_{\text{new}} = \frac{12599.6}{1.025} = 12292.3 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
BM_{\text{new}} = \frac{29268.3 - 2000}{12292.3} = \frac{27268.3}{12292.3} = 2.218 \text{ m}
$$

**New KB** (using $KB \approx 0.53\,T$): $KB_{\text{new}} \approx 0.53 \times 6.39 = 3.387$ m.

**Residual GM**

$$
GM_{\text{res}} = 3.387 + 2.218 - 6.361 = -0.756 \text{ m}
$$

**(iii) SOLAS Check**

| Criterion | Limit | Result | Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Margin line not submerged | ML = 6.5 m above keel; WL = 6.39 m | Clearance = 0.11 m | **PASS** |
| Positive GM (necessary for any righting moment) | $> 0$ | −0.756 m | **FAIL** |
| Range $\geq 7^\circ$ | $\geq 7^\circ$ | N/A (no range) | **FAIL** |

**Conclusion:** The loading condition is **NOT acceptable**. The deck is just above water but the ship has no positive residual stability → capsize risk. Redesign action: lower $KG_{\text{intact}}$ to ~5.5 m (reduce deck cargo or use ballast), or sub-divide the compartment. [7 + 7 = 14 marks]

---

> **Q.3(b) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, ESE Module 2 Choice A]**  
> **(i)** Explain the **"Lost Buoyancy"** method of damage stability calculation. Why does the added-layer method become inaccurate for large floods?  
> **(ii)** A bulk carrier is damaged in a double-bottom tank. Compartment details: $V_{\text{compt}}$ = 1 500 m³, $\mu$ = 0.95, $k_{g,f}$ = 0.4 m. Ship data: $T$ = 8.0 m, $A_W$ = 1 800 m², $KG$ = 7.0 m, $KB$ = 4.4 m, $BM$ = 3.0 m, $\Delta$ = 18 000 t. Compute the new mean draft and the residual $GM$.

**Model Solution:**

**(i)** In the *Lost Buoyancy* method, the flooded compartment is assumed to provide **no net upward force** on the ship because the seawater inside and outside exerts equal hydrostatic pressure on the compartment boundaries. The ship then floats on the *reduced intact volume* — i.e., the intact displaced volume minus the flooded volume. The new draft is found by:

$$
T_{\text{new}} : \text{such that } \nabla(T_{\text{new}}) = \nabla_{\text{intact}} - \mu V_{\text{compt}}
$$

The added-layer method assumes $A_W$ is constant and the change in draft is small, giving $T_{\text{new}} \approx T + \mu V/A_W$. This is **inaccurate for large floods** because: (a) $A_W$ changes substantially with draft, (b) the waterplane shape may not be linear, and (c) the block coefficient $C_B$ at the deeper draft differs. The lost-buoyancy method requires **iterative** use of the ship's hydrostatic curves and is the standard for SOLAS certification. [7 marks]

**(ii)**

$$
w_f = 1.025 \times 0.95 \times 1500 = 1460.6 \text{ t}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \frac{0.95 \times 1500}{1800} = \frac{1425}{1800} = 0.792 \text{ m}
$$

$$
T_{\text{new}} = 8.0 + 0.792 = 8.792 \text{ m}
$$

$$
KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{7.0 \times 18000 + 0.4 \times 1460.6}{19460.6} = \frac{126000 + 584.2}{19460.6} = 6.500 \text{ m}
$$

For a double-bottom tank the waterplane removal is small; assume $I_{\text{removed}} \approx 0$.

$$
I_{\text{intact}} = 3.0 \times \frac{18000}{1.025} = 52683 \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
BM_{\text{new}} = \frac{52683}{19460.6/1.025} = \frac{52683}{18986} = 2.775 \text{ m}
$$

$$
KB_{\text{new}} = 0.535 \times 8.792 = 4.704 \text{ m}
$$

$$
GM_{\text{res}} = 4.704 + 2.775 - 6.500 = 0.979 \text{ m}
$$

**Result:** $T_{\text{new}} = 8.79$ m, $GM_{\text{res}} = +0.98$ m → ship survives with positive residual GM. [7 marks]

### 5.3 Part B — Long-Answer Question B (14 Marks, alternative)

> **Q.4(a) [KTU University Exam — May 2023, ESE Module 2 Choice B]**  
> **(i)** With a neat sketch, describe the **three stages of progressive flooding** of a damaged compartment.  
> **(ii)** A chemical tanker has $L$ = 110 m, $B$ = 16 m, $T$ = 4.5 m, $A_W$ = 1 300 m², $\Delta$ = 7 500 t, $KG$ = 5.0 m, $BM$ = 2.0 m. Damage occurs in a midship side compartment: $V_{\text{compt}}$ = 600 m³, $\mu$ = 0.70, $k_{g,f}$ = 2.4 m, $I_{\text{removed}} = 1\,100$ m⁴. Compute the residual GM and the range of positive residual stability.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Three Stages of Progressive Flooding** (with sketch in 4.1)

1. **Initial Flooding:** Water enters through breach; compartment is partially full. The air inside is pressurised and vents through the air-pipe until equilibrium with the outside water pressure.
2. **Plunging/Final Flooding:** Once the air-escape is complete, water fills the compartment up to the outside waterline.
3. **Equilibrium Reached:** The compartment is fully filled, the ship has sunk/trammed to the new equilibrium waterline, and the GZ curve is re-computed for the damaged configuration. [7 marks]

**(ii)**

$$
w_f = 1.025 \times 0.70 \times 600 = 430.5 \text{ t},\quad \Delta_{\text{new}} = 7930.5 \text{ t}
$$

$$
\Delta T = \frac{0.70 \times 600}{1300} = 0.323 \text{ m}\quad\Rightarrow\quad T_{\text{new}} = 4.823 \text{ m}
$$

$$
KG_{\text{new}} = \frac{5.0 \times 7500 + 2.4 \times 430.5}{7930.5} = \frac{37500 + 1033.2}{7930.5} = 4.859 \text{ m}
$$

$$
I_{\text{intact}} = 2.0 \times 7317 = 14634 \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
\nabla_{\text{new}} = 7930.5/1.025 = 7737 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
BM_{\text{new}} = \frac{14634 - 1100}{7737} = 1.750 \text{ m}
$$

$$
KB_{\text{new}} = 0.535 \times 4.823 = 2.580 \text{ m}
$$

$$
GM_{\text{res}} = 2.580 + 1.750 - 4.859 = -0.529 \text{ m}
$$

The **negative residual GM** means the ship has **no positive range** — it capsizes. Range = 0°. [7 marks]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$** when computing $w_f$ and $\nabla$. Many students use 1.0 → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Using intact $KB$ instead of $KB_{\text{new}}$** at the new draft → lose 1.5 marks.
> 3. **Reporting $KG_{\text{new}}$ as "stability"** — the correct term is $GM_{\text{residual}} = KB + BM - KG$.
> 4. **Ignoring the I-removed term** (waterplane correction) → up to 2 marks lost.
> 5. **Failing to verify the Margin Line submersion** (SOLAS final check) → 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing "permeability" with "permeable length"** — they are unrelated concepts.
> 7. **Reporting negative range as "0° to 0°"** instead of stating *no range exists* — wording matters for full credit.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Damage Stability at a Given Loading Condition**

- ☐ **Always start with hydrostatics at the intact load condition** — record $L$, $B$, $T$, $A_W$, $\Delta$, $KG$, $KB$, $BM$, $I_{WL}$.
- ☐ **Identify the damaged compartment(s)** and the **permeability** (μ) from the standard SOLAS table.
- ☐ **Compute flooded water mass** $w_f = \rho_{sw} \cdot \mu \cdot V$ with $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³ (sea water).
- ☐ **New mean draft:** $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{intact}} + (\mu V) / A_W$ (added-layer method) **or** iterate via lost-buoyancy.
- ☐ **New $KG$:** moment balance — flood water is a "rigid" added mass at its centroid $k_{g,f}$.
- ☐ **New $BM$:** $I_{new} = I_{intact} - I_{removed}$; $BM_{new} = I_{new} / \nabla_{new}$.
- ☐ **New $KB$:** read from hydrostatic curves at $T_{new}$, or estimate $\approx 0.53\,T_{new}$.
- ☐ **Residual GM:** $\boxed{GM_{\text{res}} = KB_{\text{new}} + BM_{\text{new}} - KG_{\text{new}}}$.
- ☐ **Residual GZ:** $GZ(\theta) = KN_{\text{intact}}(\theta) - KG_{\text{new}} \sin\theta$.
- ☐ **Verify SOLAS criteria** in this strict order: (1) margin line, (2) range ≥ 7°, (3) max GZ ≥ 0.10 m between 20°–30°, (4) area to 20° ≥ 0.017 m·rad, (5) area 30°–40° ≥ 0.009 m·rad, (6) total area to 40° ≥ 0.040 m·rad (cargo).
- ☐ **If $GM_{\text{res}} \le 0$** → ship capsizes, report as FAIL with a redesign suggestion (sub-divide, lower $KG$, de-water by pumping).
- ☐ **Common constant:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³, margin line = **0.0762 m** above the subdivision loadline, permeability range **0.60 – 0.95**.
- ☐ **Symbolic shortcut:** $\Delta T$ depends only on $\mu V$ and $A_W$ — independent of $\rho_{sw}$ in the added-layer method.
- ☐ **Always** draw the damaged cross-section, label the flood water centroid, the new WL, and the margin line in the answer script — KTU examiners award partial marks for clear diagrams even when the math is incomplete.

> **Final Word:** Damage stability is the *experiment* in the lab because it integrates nearly every prior naval-architecture concept — hydrostatics, GZ curves, trim, permeability, regulatory rules — into a single numerical verdict: **survives or doesn't**. Memorise the 12 formulas in Section 2.3, master the 10-step computation matrix in Section 3.4, and you will secure full marks on this experiment.
